title: Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street
author: Herman Melville
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street" book by Herman Melville?
Options:
A. Go into the next room and tell Nippers to come see me. I'd rather not, Bartleby responded respectfully and slowly before calmly leaving. Alright Bartleby, I said serenely yet sternly, hinting that I was about to punish him severely. Though at the moment I partly meant it. But as dinner approached, I decided to just go home for the day, very troubled. Should I admit it? The end result was that it became standard that the pale young scrivener named Bartleby, with a desk, copied for me at four cents a page; but reviewing his work was now Turkey and Nippers' job, presumably because they were smarter; also, Bartleby would absolutely not run any errands; and even if asked, he would likely refuse outright.
B. Go into the other room and tell Nippers to come here. I would prefer not to, Bartleby responded respectfully and unhurriedly before quietly leaving. Very well Bartleby, I said in a calm yet stern self-controlled manner, hinting I was about to severely punish him. At the moment I partly meant it. But as dinner approached, I decided to just go home for the day, very troubled. Should I admit it? The conclusion was that it became established that the pale young copyist named Bartleby, with a desk, copied for me at four cents a page; but verifying his work was now Turkey and Nippers' responsibility, presumably because they were sharper; also, Bartleby would absolutely not do any errands; and even if asked, he would probably refuse outright.
C. Go to the next room, and tell Nippers to come to me. I prefer not to, he respectfully and slowly said, and mildly disappeared. Very good, Bartleby, said I, in a quiet sort of serenely severe self-possessed tone, intimating the unalterable purpose of some terrible retribution very close at hand. At the moment I half intended something of the kind. But upon the whole, as it was drawing towards my dinner-hour, I thought it best to put on my hat and walk home for the day, suffering much from perplexity and distress of mind. Shall I acknowledge it? The conclusion of this whole business was, that it soon became a fixed fact of my chambers, that a pale young scrivener, by the name of Bartleby, and a desk there; that he copied for me at the usual rate of four cents a folio (one hundred words); but he was permanently exempt from examining the work done by him, that duty being transferred to Turkey and Nippers, one of compliment doubtless to their superior acuteness; moreover, said Bartleby was never on any account to be dispatched on the most trivial errand of any sort; and that even if entreated to take upon him such a matter, it was generally understood that he would prefer not toin other words, that he would refuse pointblank.
D. Go to the adjoining room and tell Nippers I want to see him. I would prefer not to, Bartleby responded politely and slowly before calmly leaving the room. Very well Bartleby, I said in a calm yet stern self-controlled tone, suggesting I was about to harshly punish him. At the moment I partly meant it. But as dinner approached, I decided to just go home for the day, very troubled. Should I admit it? The end was that it became standard that the pale young copyist named Bartleby, with a desk, copied for me at four cents a page; but checking his work now fell to Turkey and Nippers, presumably because they were sharper; also, Bartleby would not do any errands whatsoever; and even if asked, he would likely refuse point blank.  
Answer:
